ending: Book 5 closes without resolving its two headline setups. The planned infiltration of the Blacktongue party to expose the Adventurer's Guild operative Mask never happens on the page, and the climax pivots entirely to the Dwarven Council. Convinced Arwin stole dwarven techniques, Councilwoman Indrana sends the warrior Gideon to kill him; Arwin's autonomous ivory golem, the Soul Guardian, defeats Gideon publicly, and Lillia captures him with overwhelming shadow magic. Where the major players stand: Arwin is unharmed at his forge, his power decisively displayed through the golem, with his new armor begun but not finished. Lillia has taken command of the aftermath, openly revealed as non-human, and is steering a direct confrontation with the Council. Gideon is captured and doomed, though his armor will be returned. Ida, having refused to call her mother for rescue, is estranged in spirit from Indrana and cooperating inside the Devil's Den. Indrana, off page, is about to be contacted directly. The Menagerie and its allies are all alive and thriving. Open threads carry into a possible book 6: the imminent Lillia-Indrana confrontation and the wider Dwarven Council conflict; the still-unexecuted Blacktongue party and the Mask reckoning; the unfinished armor and Koyu's promised body; the legendary vault still unlocated; Eleven's owed explanation of class-sunsetting; Vix's uncured heart; and the deeper mysteries of the dying world, Arwin's growing Hungering Maw, and the Setting Sun's tie to his and Lillia's summoned past and his charge to find One.
in_short: Rise of the Living Forge 5 follows Arwin Tyrr, the presumed-dead Hero of Lian, working as the masked smith Ifrit and leading the guild Menagerie. At the Adept Proving Grounds, two teams he equipped fight through the tournament: Phoenix Circle reaches the final and, though defeated, saves the dying undead Elias and stops the power-thief Hein, whom Kien kills; and Thornhelm withdraws to spare Kien from execution. The mysterious rival smith Necrohammer is revealed as Norman, Phoenix Circle's vanished founder, who then tells Arwin the world's secret: it is dying, the Guild manufactures war to feed the Mesh with the deaths of summoned heroes, and he saved Arwin and Lillia to break the cycle. Home in Milten, Arwin allies loosely with the Setting Sun, learns soulmancy from the lich Koyu and the dwarf Wallace, and begins building an armor to house his sentient smithy. He clears an expert dungeon to save the noble boy Thane and win a legendary vault-key. The book ends when the Dwarven Councilwoman Indrana sends the warrior Gideon to kill Ifrit: Arwin's autonomous ivory Soul Guardian golem overpowers him, and Lillia captures him with shadow magic, setting up a direct confrontation with the Dwarven Council that carries into the next book.

recaps.text: Previously, across the earlier books: Arwin Tyrr, the presumed-dead Hero of Lian, survived the explosion that ended a secretly staged war and was cast into the remote city of Milten, reborn with the unique Living Forge crafting class and a curse, the Hungering Maw, that forces him to devour magic to live. Working behind the mask of the smith Ifrit, he built a small guild, the Menagerie, around the tavern of Lillia, the former Demon Queen, likewise stripped of her power and now running the Devil's Den. The guild grew to include the thief-turned-Warden Reya, the fallen paladin Rodrick, the healer Anna, the one-armed warrior Olive, the vampire merchant Madiv, and the curiosity-shop dealer Esmerelda. Arwin rose through the tiers, took up soul-forging, and bonded to a sentient smithy, the Infernal Armory, and an evolved living hammer. He allied with a centuries-old lich, Koyu, who warned that the world itself is slowly starving. The Menagerie destroyed the masked assassin Twelve, drawing the notice of the numbered organization he served, the Setting Sun. Invited to a tournament, the Adept Proving Grounds, the guild outfitted two teams: the struggling Phoenix Circle, whose fighters include Olive, the bandaged and secretive Elias, and the non-human Maeve; and the Thornhelm team of the strategist Art, his dying sister Vix, and Kien, a renowned adventurer whose power was stolen by his brother Hein. Book 4 ended at the tournament's opening ceremony, before a single match, with Hein competing using Kien's stolen strength and the Menagerie under fresh outside observation.

So far this book: the Proving Grounds' group-stage melees begin, and Arwin watches from a spectator balcony with Reya, unable to fight for fear the Secret Eye will recognize the Hero of Lian. He spots a two-person team bearing the Setting Sun's silver insignia - a bald, unarmed monk who knocks opponents' spirits loose and a silent staff-wielder - and sends Reya, carrying an item that lets her walk through walls, to scout the field. Esmerelda works the stands in disguise; using a potion that reads life force, she foresees that a poor competitor's son, Arnold, is fated to die, and asks Arwin to help prevent it. Arwin also studies a trio in flawless obsidian dwarven-forged armor and ties them to the rival smith the Secret Eye warned him about. Olive takes the arena for Phoenix Circle and, in her melee, comes face to face with her old team - Jason, Damien, and Bea, the people tied to the loss of her arm, now richly equipped. Shaken but resolute, she defeats all three alone without her signature ability, severing Bea's arm and walking away with hard-won closure.

Reya scouts the tournament openly, unnerving the Secret Eye's observers, who note she walked clean through a warded wall and ignored a bag of tracked gold left as a test. She locates Arnold, plants false intelligence with a rival spy - that Olive's wooden arm is a weakness to be struck - and finds Esmerelda shadowing her to handle the boy herself. Art's team wins its melee carried by Kien, who fights with overwhelming strength and his broom-shaped weapon while hiding his regained power. Arwin confirms that Kien's brother, Hein, is competing alone, carrying a dagger that harms through bare flesh and resists steel, and arranges to warn Olive and Kien. Esmerelda saves Arnold with a limited, costly boost. The melees end and the first proper team round pits Phoenix Circle against the Carreras Coalition, which fields a trio of Falling Blade assassins. Olive kills all three, her cursed arm surging with bloodlust and briefly seizing control to finish the last on its own. The Menagerie advances, and Olive is unsettled by how little the killing moved her.

A Setting Sun operative, Eleven, watches from a rented room, disturbed by Olive's executions and the near-sentient bloodlust of her arm; she is scouting for recruits whose classes can be 'sun-set.' Starforge, led by the gravity mage Ursa, is revealed as the enemy that has been killing Art's family and trying to seize Thornhelm. When the teams meet, Art demonstrates his true power - he can copy and nullify an opponent's magic, storing it in cards - and strips Ursa of his gravity magic. Kien holds off Starforge's synchronized twins, Lily and Ash, alone at Art's insistence, so the team can prove itself. Cornered by Ursa's soul-armor, Art uses a time-freezing ability to plan an escape, then releases a power sealed inside his sister: Vix unleashes invisible, lethal high-speed strikes that carve through the armor and drives a dagger into Ursa's skull, killing him, at the cost of a spreading corruption Art must reseal. The name of the smith behind Starforge's soul-armor surfaces - Necrohammer - and Thornhelm advances, leaving a second Menagerie-outfitted team with dead aggressors in its wake.

Eleven recognizes that the Menagerie deliberately equipped two teams and privately identifies Vix as a warlock bound to her brother Art, revealing herself to be a warlock too when she fails to recruit Kien. Arwin and Reya puzzle over Necrohammer, an evidently masterful smith whose gear performs poorly in real combat; they conclude he has spread single overpowered pieces across nearly a dozen teams as a reputation play. Meanwhile Esmerelda's intervention for Arnold plays out: she had given the doomed boy a cursed sword with a warning never to draw it. When an enemy knight overpowers Arnold's team and draws the blade himself, the weapon's bound devil turns on him and reduces him to dust, sparing Arnold, who refuses to lean on its stolen power, loses the bout, but survives. The reader learns Esmerelda acts as a 'dealer' who places cursed weapons but cannot choose their wielders. The quarterfinals open, matching Phoenix Circle against Blackguard, the pitch-black-armored team Arwin believes Necrohammer fully outfitted.

Phoenix Circle faces Blackguard in the quarterfinal. Olive fights the honorable Vale and the fire-armored second Blackguard fighter two-on-one; deafened by Vale's shrieking sword, she deliberately defies her own sword-purist Challenge without being punished for it, then unlocks the seal on her Ifrit armor, revealing it to be a soul weapon. Her cursed arm gains a voice and bargains for shared control, and Arwin lends her his living hammer, Caldera, for the single fight. Olive's adaptive armor and Maeve's ability to fully deactivate enemy gear win the match; the defeated Vale entrusts Olive with a hidden plain dagger. Studying it, Arwin finds it is a living weapon forged from human finger-bone, twin to Hein's power-stealing dagger, and pieces together that Necrohammer sacrificed his own fingers to build a counter-weapon meant to destroy Hein's. In private, Necrohammer is revealed at last: he is Norman, Phoenix Circle's vanished dwarf-necromancer founder, long ago cast out by the Dwarven Council. His undead fighter Elias is his first and proudest creation, now dying, and Norman means to shatter the daggers and spend their stolen power to save him.

The counter-dagger reaches Art's team just as the semifinal pits them against Hein. Kien unmasks before his stunned brother and, with Art secretly freezing time and feeding his own energy to buy a single decisive moment, beats Hein down; Vix rips the dagger from Hein's hand and Kien clashes the twin blades together, shattering both. Stripped of his stolen power, Hein formally surrenders to hide behind Kien's hero morality - and Kien kills him anyway, punching through his heart and declaring he is no longer a hero. Facing execution by the Adventurer's Guild for the post-surrender kill, Kien intends to die so Art and Vix can go on; Art instead withdraws Thornhelm from the tournament to save him. When a Guild axeman moves to kill Art, Arwin breaks his cover, hurls Caldera, and reveals himself as Ifrit, while Necrohammer publicly backs a lie that the cursed dagger controlled Kien. The Secret Eye accepts Thornhelm's resignation and closes the matter. In private, Necrohammer - dying and out of time - takes the unconscious Elias and works on him with the black gem set in his palm, calling the undead a 'piece' that needs only finishing touches.

The final pits Phoenix Circle against Setting Sun, who field a hidden third member - a forgettable shadow-mage who has scouted all tournament and now targets Olive's arm, exactly as Reya's false intelligence intended, so a blow that should have ended the fight instead glances off her toughest part. Elias, secretly restored by Necrohammer, at last fights with real power and rings himself out along with the near-invincible monk to remove him. Olive, cornered and offered victory by her cursed arm if she will only kill, refuses and is knocked out instead, holding to who she is. Maeve, hearing that Elias's magical 'song' has fully returned, understands Norman truly cured him rather than merely patching him; she eliminates the enemy mage but is then kicked out of bounds, and Setting Sun wins the tournament. Yet Phoenix Circle achieves every real goal: Elias is safe and no longer dying, Hein is stopped, the assassins who targeted the guild are dead, and the Menagerie's craft is proven. The tournament arc closes with the teams parting in mutual respect.

With the tournament over, Necrohammer arranges a private meeting and tells Arwin the truth about the world. He knows Arwin was summoned from another world, and reveals that whole peoples - the dwarves, demons, and more - were mass-summoned long ago. The world is dying, starved of magical energy, and the Mesh harvests a share of every life and death; the endless war exists to feed it. The Adventurer's Guild summons powerful heroes and demon queens, lets them grow strong, then kills them, because a fully realized high-tier class yields the most energy when its holder dies - and any hero who cared to protect the world was killed to keep the war going. Lillia, too, was summoned from another planet, and neither she nor Arwin ever chose their class. Necrohammer reveals it was he who planted the black gem in Arwin's armor, a device that detonated to strip Arwin's magic and halt the rare 'sunsetting' of his class, saving him and Lillia from the Guild's harvest to break the cycle and buy the world time. He urges Arwin to grow stronger, to seek out the figure called One, and to find a way to save a dying world, then departs.

The Menagerie returns to Milten, where the Devil's Den has thrived on tournament crowds and Lillia's growing Hearthkeeper power. Arwin tells the whole guild everything Necrohammer disclosed. The Setting Sun's Eleven arrives at the tavern, probes the guild about Twelve's death, and - impressed that no one panics - pivots from recruitment to an alliance: rather than absorb the Menagerie, One wants a standing, non-exclusive arrangement in which the guild crafts for and works alongside the Setting Sun, gaining access to its dungeons and, eventually, instruction in sunsetting classes. She leaves behind a dungeon heart as a gift and a show of strength. Thornhelm and Phoenix Circle formally attach themselves to the Menagerie; Elias and Maeve stay on, Elias now permanently made whole by a 'monster core' Necrohammer built from Hein's stolen power. Esmerelda recognizes Vix as a warlock, though the guild sets the matter aside. Arwin resolves to fix Vix's heart, possibly through the dungeon heart or her warlock bond to Art, and returns to the forge intent on building himself a powerful new suit of armor before seeking out One.

Arwin settles back into Milten as the Menagerie grows wealthy and famous. He and Lillia plan an auction house for the city, to be run by Madiv, and the guild runs dungeons to stock the smithy. Working with the Infernal Armory, Arwin conceives his flagship project: not merely a suit of armor but one that can house the sentient Armory itself, letting it leave its building and fight at his side. Koyu appears in person as a lich and reacts with fear to the word 'soulmancy' - the ability Arwin unknowingly gained when he made his Soul Guardian. Koyu explains that soulmancy is the art of enslaving a soul, practiced by history's worst liches, and is horrified until he learns Arwin reached it by a wholly different path: genuinely communing with and contracting the willing souls of his materials. He shares a memory of his ancient master, the greater lich Shade, who forged a soul-bound weapon that once ended a war by destroying everything, including itself, and warns that Arwin's own bow may descend from it. Koyu and the dwarven smith Wallace both agree to mentor Arwin, and the three begin training together toward the armor, Arwin advancing to Adept Two.

Arwin tells the entire Menagerie his full origin, and the guild shares its own secrets until nothing is hidden among them. Melissa brings a job: escort the untrained teenage son of the wealthy House Blacktongue into an expert-tier dungeon so the boy can earn a class, with access to the family's crystal mines as the real prize. The boy, Thane, proves to be an odd, humble, obsessive researcher who has calmly concluded he will die in the delve. Arwin, liking the boy but despising the family, resolves to keep Thane alive and to strip the dungeon bare in spite. With Koyu and Wallace, Arwin forges a disposable prototype of his armor - a thin skeleton and bracelets meant to test housing the Infernal Armory remotely - knowing it will break under strain. He assembles an elite three-fighter party of himself, Olive, and Anna, and at the dungeon's threshold uses a newfound power to offer the classless Thane a mesh 'quest,' declaring the fearful boy reborn as one who will never throw his life away.

Deep in the Blacktongue dungeon, Arwin clears rooms with overwhelming force while shielding Thane, who is forbidden to fight. A first boss falls to a single charged arrow, and an entire room of monsters cowers rather than attack. At the final chamber, a hive-mother - an egg-laying bat-and-worm creature surrounded by hatching young - proves un-flankable because it shares its senses with its brood; Thane cracks the puzzle, and by killing hatchlings to disrupt the link the party creates the opening for Arwin to end it with a full-power strike, remotely summoning the Infernal Armory through his prototype armor for the first time. The prototype breaks exactly as predicted, its job done. The Menagerie claims the dungeon's first clear, far beyond what House Blacktongue expected. Thane, who entered certain he would die, breaks down with relief, then discovers he truly earned a class - 'Combat Archivist,' which lets him learn ever more about an item or monster the longer he studies it. Among the loot is a heavily rusted circlet, so encrusted that its magic reads as nothing at all, which the party keeps to examine later. Arwin sends House Blacktongue his regards.

Back in Milten, Arwin and the Infernal Armory design a second, layered version of the armor around a protected core. The challenger smith who has camped outside for weeks finally forces a confrontation and is revealed as Ida, a Dwarven Master Smith and daughter of the Councilwoman Indrana, sent to discover which dwarf illegally taught Arwin his craft. Arwin wins a wager by devouring her master's hammer with his Hungering Maw, hiding his mentor Wallace and humiliating her, though it deepens the Dwarven Council's interest. Eleven returns and offers a new job: infiltrate a coming-of-age party at House Blacktongue, where the Adventurer's Guild is sending an operative called Mask - the man the Setting Sun believes gave Hein the cursed dagger. Arwin agrees, trading Eleven sanctioned observation of the guild for shared intelligence, and resolves to build the new armor within three days and test it against Mask. Reviewing the dungeon loot, the guild confirms the Legendary Band of the Vault Keeper points to a hidden vault that could solve their material needs - and that the Blacktongues do not yet know what they lost.

As the Menagerie prepares to infiltrate the Blacktongue party, Esmerelda produces a genuine signed invitation, secured by her will-driven shop through Thane, who has begun working for her. Pressed on why she keeps placing cursed items despite promising to stop, Esmerelda reveals her true class: her trade is not goods but people, redistributing dangerous items to suitable wielders and keeping the worst from the undeserving, and the guild concludes she has quietly averted disasters for years. Eleven names her 'the Curse Broker' and confirms the Setting Sun tracks everyone whose class has been sunsetted, of whom the Menagerie now holds several. A crate of extraordinary high-grade materials arrives as commission payment, including a room-temperature liquid metal ideal for the armor's flexible interior and a crimson battle-metal that pulls Arwin into a vision of a bloodied, bodiless battlefield and then refuses to communicate. With Wallace and Koyu, Arwin begins forging the most complex armor he has ever attempted, the three minds linked through the Infernal Armory, the day before the party.

While Arwin, Wallace, and Koyu are deep in forging the new armor, the Blacktongue infiltration never happens. Instead, Ida reports to her mother by telepathic stone that Arwin is 'something else entirely,' not dwarven-trained; refusing to believe it, Indrana sends the warrior Gideon - clad in her own high-grade armor and offered Ida's hand in marriage as reward - to kill Ifrit. At the smithy, Arwin's autonomous protective golem, the hollow ivory Soul Guardian animated by crimson magic, awakens and defends the shop. It out-fights the expert-tier Gideon, survives being beheaded because it is empty inside, and bites chunks from his armor and arm with a hungering shark-toothed maw. Before Gideon can make a final effort, Lillia emerges from the Devil's Den, seizes him with overwhelming shadow magic, and drags him inside. Gideon is captured, defeated, and will not leave alive, though his armor will be returned. Ida, refusing to summon her mother for rescue and effectively defecting in spirit, is brought before Lillia, who reveals her fangs and orders Ida to call Indrana on the stone. The book ends there: Lillia leans in and says the three of them are going to have a talk, the direct Menagerie-versus-Dwarven-Council confrontation set up but unresolved.
